intro: The clearest split between Tink and Prove is how they charge: Tink uses subscription pricing, while Prove uses custom enterprise pricing (no public tier). Tink is European open banking platform providing bank account aggregation, payment initiation, and income verification APIs; it fits best for Fintech, Marketplace. Prove is Prove is a digital identity platform using phone-number intelligence, possession checks and Prove Pre-Fill to verify and authenticate users, cutting fraud and friction in onboarding for banks and fintechs; it fits best for US banks and fintechs using phone possession to reduce fraud and autofill KYC fields. Pick Tink when the job maps to fintech requiring identity verification; pick Prove when it maps to phone-number intelligence for frictionless kyc onboarding. Tink is not for consumer apps without regulatory requirements. Prove is not for global or privacy-minimal products that cannot rely on us carrier data for verification. Honest trade-offs: Tink is weak for consumer apps without regulatory requirements, and Prove is weak for us-centric, weaker outside north america — match to your real scenario.
quick_take: Tink is for fintech, marketplace; Prove is for us banks and fintechs using phone possession to reduce fraud; decide based on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Tink if you want subscription pricing and your workload matches fintech, marketplace.
verdict_b: Choose Prove if you want custom enterprise pricing (no public tier) and your workload matches us banks and fintechs using phone possession to reduce fraud and autofill kyc fields.

faq.a: Tink has more transparent pricing; Prove's cost depends on a sales conversation, so a direct comparison is not possible without a quote. Tink uses subscription pricing with no free tier published. Prove uses custom enterprise pricing with no published rates.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Tink to Prove?
faq.a: Migration may be non-trivial: Prove is rated high lock-in (Custom identity contracts). Plan for data export, re-integration of SDKs, and re-testing flows before cutover. Verify exportable formats and feature parity against your live use cases before committing.

Both score 4/5 on developer experience in our data. Evaluate against your own stack: read their docs, run a quick SDK spike, and check error handling. Self-assessing with a small prototype beats trusting any rating at face value.

faq.q: Is Prove a good alternative to Tink?
faq.a: Prove sits in the same category as Tink, so it can be a reasonable alternative for overlapping use cases. Prove is best fit for us banks and fintechs using phone possession to reduce fraud and autofill kyc fields. Note: Prove is not for global or privacy-minimal products that cannot rely on us carrier data for verification.
